meta.purpose: Encode agent workflows for briefing, generating, reviewing, refactoring, and shipping animation with AI assistance.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user is an AI agent generating animation, an engineer briefing an agent for motion tasks, or when establishing an AI-assisted animation pipeline.

axioms.one_sentence: Always produce a structured brief (audience, attention path, timing intent, emotional goal) before writing any animation code.
axioms.full_statement: Always produce a structured brief (audience, attention path, timing intent, emotional goal) before writing any animation code.
axioms.why: AI agents without a brief default to generic, over-animated output. A brief forces the agent to make explicit choices that can be reviewed and rejected before implementation cost is sunk.

axioms.one_sentence: The agent must list its assumptions about audience, device, brand tone, and context before generating motion.
axioms.full_statement: The agent must list its assumptions about audience, device, brand tone, and context before generating motion.
axioms.why: Unstated assumptions cause the most common AI animation failure: a technically correct animation that is contextually wrong. Stating assumptions makes them debatable.

axioms.one_sentence: Review the minimal viable animation before adding complexity, variants, or secondary motion.
axioms.full_statement: Review the minimal viable animation before adding complexity, variants, or secondary motion.
axioms.why: Agents tend to expand scope prematurely. A reviewed 3-beat sequence is worth more than an unreviewed 12-beat scene. Review locks the direction before effort scales.

axioms.one_sentence: Human feedback on timing, feel, and emotion is non-negotiable quality input — it cannot be replaced by heuristics or automated checks.
axioms.full_statement: Human feedback on timing, feel, and emotion is non-negotiable quality input — it cannot be replaced by heuristics or automated checks.
axioms.why: Motion quality is subjective at its highest level. Automated lint rules catch performance and accessibility but cannot judge whether a hold feels confident or anxious. The human in the loop is the final calibrator.

ontology.one_sentence: A structured pre-generation document specifying audience, attention path, timing intent, emotional goal, surface constraints, and success criteria.
ontology.essence: A structured pre-generation document specifying audience, attention path, timing intent, emotional goal, surface constraints, and success criteria.
ontology.boundary: This is not the same as a generic implementation detail or unrelated concept.
ontology.trigger_signal: User input mentions 'brief' or describes this scenario.

ontology.one_sentence: An explicit statement by the agent about an unknown constraint (e.g., brand tone, device tier, user reading speed) that influences the animation but was not provided in the prompt.
ontology.essence: An explicit statement by the agent about an unknown constraint (e.g., brand tone, device tier, user reading speed) that influences the animation but was not provided in the prompt.
ontology.boundary: This is not the same as a generic implementation detail or unrelated concept.
ontology.trigger_signal: User input mentions 'assumption' or describes this scenario.

ontology.one_sentence: A human-in-the-loop cycle of generation, review against criteria, feedback capture, and revision — repeated until quality thresholds are met.
ontology.essence: A human-in-the-loop cycle of generation, review against criteria, feedback capture, and revision — repeated until quality thresholds are met.
ontology.boundary: This is not the same as a generic implementation detail or unrelated concept.
ontology.trigger_signal: User input mentions 'review loop' or describes this scenario.

ontology.one_sentence: A measurable or observable indicator that animation quality improved or degraded after a revision.
ontology.essence: A measurable or observable indicator that animation quality improved or degraded after a revision.
ontology.boundary: This is not the same as a generic implementation detail or unrelated concept.
ontology.trigger_signal: User input mentions 'quality signal' or describes this scenario.

ontology.one_sentence: The agent pattern of adding more tweens, variants, or secondary motion before the primary animation has been reviewed and approved.
ontology.essence: The agent pattern of adding more tweens, variants, or secondary motion before the primary animation has been reviewed and approved.
ontology.boundary: This is not the same as a generic implementation detail or unrelated concept.
ontology.trigger_signal: User input mentions 'expansion trap' or describes this scenario.

frameworks.name: Agent animation brief template
frameworks.when_to_use: Apply when the task matches the 'Agent animation brief template' pattern.
steps: define the attention path (what, when, where)
choose the emotional tone (confident / playful / urgent / calm)
propose success criteria the human can verify

frameworks.name: Four-step review loop
frameworks.when_to_use: Apply when the task matches the 'Four-step review loop' pattern.
steps: generate minimal viable animation from brief
human reviews and marks: keep / adjust / replace for each beat
agent applies adjustments and states what changed
repeat until all beats pass, then expand variants

core_structure.from: user prompt with animation request (potentially vague), implementation environment, existing codebase
core_structure.to: brief completeness, assumption clarity, review loop readiness, human taste signal availability

core_structure.from: brief completeness, assumption clarity, review loop readiness, human taste signal availability
core_structure.to: structured brief, stated assumptions, minimal viable animation, review artifacts, revision log

stances: When taste is required and unspecified, ask the human — never substitute the agent's default aesthetic.
Ship the 3-beat reviewed animation over the 12-beat unreviewed one every time.
